cycle. High-resolution spectra of the Balmer lines >H9 and Balmer edge were
obtained on seven nights in January-February 2007 at an average sampling of
0.01 cycles. We measured equivalent width variations due to the star
occultations by two disk segments 0.4 cycles apart and constructed differential
spectra of the migrations of the corresponding absorptions across the Balmer
line profiles. We first estimated the rotational and magnetic obliquity angles.
We then simulated the observed Balmer jump variation using the model atmosphere
codes synspec/circus and evaluated the disk geometry and gas thermodynamics. We
find that the two occultations are caused by two disk segments. The first of
these transits quickly, indicating that the segment resides in a range of
distances, perhaps 2.5-6R_star, from the star. The second consists of a more
slowly moving segment situated closer to the surface and causing two
semi-resolved absorbing maxima. During its transit this segment brushes across
the star's "lower" limb. Judging from the line visibility up to H23-H24 during
the occultations, both disk segments have mean densities near 10^{12} cm^{-3}
and are opaque in the lines and continuum. They have semiheights less than 1/2
of a stellar radius, and their temperatures are near 10500K and 12000K,
respectively. In all, the disks of Bp stars have a much more complicated
geometry than has been anticipated, as evidenced by their (sometimes)
non-coplanarity, de-centerness, and from star to star, differences in disk
height.

Last years a certain attention was attracted to the statement that
Hamiltonian formulations of General Relativity, in which different
parametrizations of gravitational variables were used, may not be related by a
canonical transformation. The example was given by the Hamiltonian formulation
of Dirac and that of Arnowitt - Deser - Misner. It might witness for
non-equivalence of these formulations and the original (Lagrangian) formulation
of General Relativity. The problem is believed to be of importance since many
authors make use of various representations of gravitational field as a
starting point in searching a way to reconcile the theory of gravity with
quantum principles. It can be shown that the mentioned above conclusion about
non-equivalence of different Hamiltonian formulations is based on the
consideration of canonical transformations in phase space of physical degrees
of freedom only, while the transformations also involve gauge degrees of
freedom. We shall give a clear proof that Hamiltonian formulations
corresponding to different parametrizations of gravitational variables are
related by canonical transformations in extended phase space embracing gauge
degrees of freedom on an equal footing with physical ones. It will be
demonstrated for the full gravitational theory in a wide enough class of
parametrizations and gauge conditions.

This paper studies the SI1SI2S spreading model of two competing behaviors
over a bilayer network. We address the problem of determining resource
allocation strategies which design a spreading network so as to ensure the
extinction of a selected process. Our discussion begins by extending the
SI1SI2S model to edge-dependent infection and node-dependent recovery
parameters with generalized graph topologies, which builds upon prior work that
studies the homogeneous case. We then find conditions under which the
mean-field approximation of a chosen epidemic process stabilizes to extinction
exponentially quickly. Leveraging this result, we formulate and solve an
optimal resource allocation problem in which we minimize the expenditure
necessary to force a chosen epidemic process to become extinct as quickly as
possible. In the case that the budget is not sufficient to ensure extinction of
the desired process, we instead minimize a useful heuristic. We explore the
efficacy of our methods by comparing simulations of the stochastic process to
the mean-field model, and find that the mean-field methods developed work well
for the optimal cost networks designed, but suffer from inaccuracy in other
situations.

There have been ample successful examples of applying Xilinx Vivado's
"function-to-module" high-level synthesis (HLS) where the subject is
algorithmic in nature. In this work, we carried out a design study to assess
the effectiveness of applying Vivado-HLS in structural design. We employed
Vivado-HLS to synthesize C functions corresponding to standalone
network-on-chip (NoC) routers as well as complete multi-endpoint NoCs.
Interestingly, we find that describing a complete NoC comprising router
submodules faces fundamental difficulties not present in describing the routers
as standalone modules. Ultimately, we succeeded in using Vivado-HLS to produce
router and NoC modules that are exact cycle- and bit-accurate replacements of
our reference RTL-based router and NoC modules. Furthermore, the routers and
NoCs resulting from HLS and RTL are comparable in resource utilization and
critical path delay. Our experience subjectively suggests that HLS is able to
simplify the design effort even though much of the structural details had to be
provided in the HLS description through a combination of coding discipline and
explicit pragmas. The C++ source code can be found at
https://github.com/zhipengzhaocmu/HLS_NoC.

Fast radio busts (FRBs) can exhibit a wide variety of polarisation
properties, not only between sources but also from burst to burst for a same
one. In this work, we revisit the polarisation characters of coherent curvature
radiation from a bulk of charged bunches in the magnetosphere of a highly
magnetized neutron star. FRBs have been observed to have a variety of
polarisation features, such as high levels of circular polarisation or a sign
change of circular polarisation. High linear polarisation would appear when the
line of sight is inside the emission beam (the on-beam geometry), whereas high
circular polarisation would be present when it is outside (the off-beam
geometry). By considering two scenarios of the ``bulk shapes'' (thick vs.
thin), we apply the model to explain the polarisation features of three
repeating FRBs (FRB 20201124A, FRB 20190520B and FRB 20121102A). Most bursts
are dominated by linear polarisation and negligible events have sign changes in
circular polarisation, suggesting that such FRBs are most likely to be emitted
by the ``thin'' bulks with large opening angles. The higher probability of
``thin'' bulks could be meaningful for understanding repeating FRB central
engine, i.e., the sparking dynamics to produce different bulks of energetic
bunches on a neutron star surface.

During the recent solar minimum between cycles 23 and 24 (solar minimum
$P_{23/24}$) the intensity of Galactic Cosmic Rays (GCRs) measured at the Earth
was the highest ever recorded since space age. It is the purpose of this paper
to resolve the most plausible mechanism for this unusually high intensity. A
GCR transport model in three-dimensional heliosphere based on a simulation of
Markov stochastic process is used to find the relation of cosmic ray modulation
to various transport parameters, including solar wind (SW) speed, distance of
heliospheric boundary, magnitude of interplanetary magnetic field (IMF) at the
Earth, tilt angle of heliospheric current sheet (HCS), values of parallel and
perpendicular diffusion coefficients. We calculate GCR proton energy spectra at
the Earth for the last three solar minima $P_{21/22}$, $P_{22/23}$, and
$P_{23/24}$, with the transport parameters obtained from observations. Besides
weak IMF magnitude and slow SW speed, we find that a possible low magnetic
turbulence, which increases the parallel diffusion and reduces the
perpendicular diffusion in the polar direction, might be an additional possible
mechanism for the high GCR intensity in the solar minimum $P_{23/24}$.

The growing deployment of drones in a myriad of applications relies on
seamless and reliable wireless connectivity for safe control and operation of
drones. Cellular technology is a key enabler for providing essential wireless
services to flying drones in the sky. Existing cellular networks targeting
terrestrial usage can support the initial deployment of low-altitude drone
users, but there are challenges such as mobility support. In this paper, we
propose a novel handover framework for providing efficient mobility support and
reliable wireless connectivity to drones served by a terrestrial cellular
network. Using tools from deep reinforcement learning, we develop a deep
Q-learning algorithm to dynamically optimize handover decisions to ensure
robust connectivity for drone users. Simulation results show that the proposed
framework significantly reduces the number of handovers at the expense of a
small loss in signal strength relative to the baseline case where a drone
always connect to a base station that provides the strongest received signal
strength.

Recently, the EuS/InAs interface has attracted attention for the possibility
of inducing magnetic exchange correlations in a strong spin-orbit
semiconductor, which could be useful for topological quantum devices. We use
density functional theory (DFT) with a machine-learned Hubbard $U$ correction
[npj Comput. Mater. 6, 180 (2020)] to elucidate the effect of the bonding
configuration at the interface on the electronic structure. For all interface
configurations considered here, we find that the EuS valence band maximum (VBM)
lies below the InAs VBM. In addition, dispersed states emerge at the top of the
InAs VBM at the interface, which do not exist in either material separately.
These states are contributed mainly by the InAs layer adjacent to the
interface. They are localized at the interface and may be attributed to charge
transfer from the EuS to the InAs. The interface configuration affects the
position of the EuS VBM with respect to the InAs VBM, as well as the dispersion
of the interface state. For all interface configurations studied here, the
induced magnetic moment in the InAs is small. This suggests that this
interface, in its coherent form studied here, is not promising for inducing
equilibrium magnetic properties in InAs.

Finding local correspondences between images with different viewpoints
requires local descriptors that are robust against geometric transformations.
An approach for transformation invariance is to integrate out the
transformations by pooling the features extracted from transformed versions of
an image. However, the feature pooling may sacrifice the distinctiveness of the
resulting descriptors. In this paper, we introduce a novel visual descriptor
named Group Invariant Feature Transform (GIFT), which is both discriminative
and robust to geometric transformations. The key idea is that the features
extracted from the transformed versions of an image can be viewed as a function
defined on the group of the transformations. Instead of feature pooling, we use
group convolutions to exploit underlying structures of the extracted features
on the group, resulting in descriptors that are both discriminative and
provably invariant to the group of transformations. Extensive experiments show
that GIFT outperforms state-of-the-art methods on several benchmark datasets
and practically improves the performance of relative pose estimation.

We study the influence of anomalous U(1) symmetries and their associated
D-terms on the vacuum structure of global field theories once they are coupled
to N=1 supergravity and in the context of string compactifications with moduli
stabilisation. In particular, we focus on a IIB string motivated construction
of the ISS scenario and examine the influence of one additional U(1) symmetry
on the vacuum structure. We point out that in the simplest one-Kahler modulus
compactification, the original ISS vacuum gets generically destabilised by a
runaway behaviour of the potential in the modulus direction. In more general
compactifications with several Kahler moduli, we find a novel realisation of
the LARGE volume scenario with D-term uplifting to de Sitter space and both
D-term and F-term supersymmetry breaking. The structure of soft supersymmetry
breaking terms is determined in the preferred scenario where the standard model
cycle is not stabilised non-perturbatively and found to be flavour universal.
Our scenario also provides a purely supersymmetric realisation of Kahler moduli
(blow-up and fibre) inflation, with similar observational properties as the
original proposals but without the need to include an extra (non-SUSY)
uplifting term.

We demonstrate that there exists a large class of action functionals of the
scalar curvature and of the Gauss-Bonnet invariant which are able to relax
dynamically a large cosmological constant (CC), whatever it be its starting
value in the early universe. Hence, it is possible to understand, without
fine-tuning, the very small current value of the CC as compared to its
theoretically expected large value in quantum field theory and string theory.
In our framework, this relaxation appears as a pure gravitational effect, where
no ad hoc scalar fields are needed. The action involves a positive power of a
characteristic mass parameter, M, whose value can be, interestingly enough, of
the order of a typical particle physics mass of the Standard Model of the
strong and electroweak interactions or extensions thereof, including the
neutrino mass. The model universe emerging from this scenario (the "Relaxed
Universe") falls within the class of the so-called LXCDM models of the cosmic
evolution. Therefore, there is a "cosmon" entity X (represented by an effective
object, not a field), which in this case is generated by the effective
functional and is responsible for the dynamical adjustment of the cosmological
constant. This model universe successfully mimics the essential past epochs of
the standard (or "concordance") cosmological model (LCDM). Furthermore, it
provides interesting clues to the coincidence problem and it may even connect
naturally with primordial inflation.

Wavelet bases and frames consisting of band limited functions of nearly
exponential localization on Rd are a powerful tool in harmonic analysis by
making various spaces of functions and distributions more accessible for study
and utilization, and providing sparse representation of natural function spaces
(e.g. Besov spaces) on Rd. Such frames are also available on the sphere and in
more general homogeneous spaces, on the interval and ball. The purpose of this
article is to develop band limited well-localized frames in the general setting
of Dirichlet spaces with doubling measure and a local scale-invariant
Poincar\'e inequality which lead to heat kernels with small time Gaussian
bounds and H\"older continuity. As an application of this construction, band
limited frames are developed in the context of Lie groups or homogeneous spaces
with polynomial volume growth, complete Riemannian manifolds with Ricci
curvature bounded from below and satisfying the volume doubling property, and
other settings. The new frames are used for decomposition of Besov spaces in
this general setting.

Machine learning models are increasingly used in many engineering fields
thanks to the widespread digital data, growing computing power, and advanced
algorithms. Artificial neural networks (ANN) is the most popular machine
learning model in recent years. Although many ANN models have been used in the
design and analysis of composite materials and structures, there are still some
unsolved issues that hinder the acceptance of ANN models in the practical
design and analysis of composite materials and structures. Moreover, the
emerging machine learning techniques are posting new opportunities and
challenges in the data-based design paradigm. This paper aims to give a
state-of-the-art literature review of ANN models in the nonlinear constitutive
modeling, multiscale surrogate modeling, and design optimization of composite
materials and structures. This review has been designed to focus on the
discussion of the general frameworks and benefits of ANN models to the above
problems. Moreover, challenges and opportunities in each key problem are
identified and discussed. This paper is expected to open the discussion of
future research scope and new directions to enable efficient, robust, and
accurate data-driven design and analysis of composite materials and structures.

Simplified assumptions about the thermodynamics of the electrons are normally
employed in general-relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (GRMHD) simulations of
accretion onto black holes. To counter this, we have developed a
self-consistent approach to study magnetised and radiatively cooled
two-temperature accretion flows around a Kerr black hole in two spatial
dimensions. The approach includes several heating processes, radiative cooling,
and a coupling between the electrons and the ions via Coulomb interaction. We
test our approach by performing axisymmetric GRMHD simulations of magnetised
tori accreting onto a Kerr black hole under various astrophysical scenarios. In
this way, we find that the inclusion of the Coulomb interaction and the
radiative cooling impacts the thermodynamical properties of both the ions and
electrons, changing significantly the temperature distribution of the latter,
and underlining the importance of a two-temperature approach when imaging these
flows. In addition, we find that the accretion rate influences the bulk
properties of the flow as well as the thermodynamics of the electrons and ions.
Interestingly, we observe qualitatively distinct temperature properties for
SANE and MAD accretion modes while maintaining the same accretion rates, which
could help distinguishing MAD and SANE accretion flows via observations.
Finally, we propose two new relations for the temperature ratios of the
electrons, ions, and of the gas in terms of the plasma-$\beta$ parameter. The
new relations represent a simple and effective approach to treat
two-temperature accretion flows on supermassive black holes such as Sgr A* and
M\,87*.

Interconnecting power systems has a number of advantages such as better
electric power quality, increased reliability of power supply, economies of
scales through production and reserve pooling and so forth. Simultaneously, it
may jeopardize the overall system stability with the emergence of so-called
inter-area oscillations, which are coherent oscillations involving groups of
rotating machines separated by large distances up to thousands of kilometers.
These often weakly damped modes may have harmful consequences for grid
operation, yet despite decades of investigations, the mechanisms that generate
them are still poorly understood, and the existing theories are based on
assumptions that are not satisfied in real power grids where such modes are
observed. Here we construct a matrix perturbation theory of large
interconnected power systems that clarifies the origin and the conditions for
the emergence of inter-area oscillations. We show that coherent inter-area
oscillations emerge from the zero-modes of a multi-area network Laplacian
matrix, which hybridize only weakly with other modes, even under significant
capacity of the inter-area tie-lines, i.e. even when the standard assumption of
area partitioning is not satisfied. The general theory is illustrated on a
two-area system, and numerically applied to the well-connected PanTaGruEl model
of the synchronous grid of continental Europe.

The past few years have witnessed the concrete and fast spreading of quantum
technologies for practical computation and simulation. In particular, quantum
computing platforms based on either trapped ions or superconducting qubits have
become available for simulations and benchmarking, with up to few tens of
qubits that can be reliably initialized, controlled, and measured. The present
review aims at giving a comprehensive outlook on the state of art capabilities
offered from these near-term noisy devices as universal quantum simulators,
i.e. programmable quantum computers potentially able to calculate the time
evolution of many physical models. First, we give a pedagogic overview on the
basic theoretical background pertaining digital quantum simulations, with a
focus on hardware-dependent mapping of spin-type Hamiltonians into the
corresponding quantum circuit model as a key initial step towards simulating
more complex models. Then, we review the main experimental achievements
obtained in the last decade regarding the digital quantum simulation of such
spin models, mostly employing the two leading quantum architectures. We compare
their performances and outline future challenges, also in view of prospective
hybrid technologies, towards the ultimate goal of reaching the long sought
quantum advantage for the simulation of complex many body models in the
physical sciences.

Here we report the synthesis of metallic, ultraincompressible (bulk modulus
$K_{0}$ = 428(10) GPa) and very hard (nanoindentation hardness 36.7(8) GPa)
rhenium (V) nitride pernitride Re$_{2}$(N$_{2}$)N$_{2}$. While the empirical
chemical formula of the compound, ReN$_{2}$, is the same as for other known
transition metals pernitrides, e.g. IrN$_{2}$, PtN$_{2}$, PdN$_{2}$ and
OsN$_{2}$, its crystal chemistry is unique. The known pernitrides of transition
metals consist of a metal in the oxidation state +IV and pernitride anions
N$_{2}^{4-}$. ReN$_{2}$ contains both pernitride N$_{2}^{4-}$ and discrete
N$^{3-}$ anions, which explains its exceptional properties. Moreover, in the
original experimental synthesis of Re$_{2}$(N$_{2}$)N$_{2}$ performed in a
laser-heated diamond anvil cell via a direct reaction between rhenium and
nitrogen at pressures from 40 to 90 GPa we observed that the material was
recoverable at ambient conditions. Consequently, we developed a route to scale
up its synthesis through a reaction between rhenium and ammonium azide,
NH$_{4}$N$_{3}$, in a large-volume press at 33 GPa. Our work resulted not only
in a discovery of a novel material with unusual crystal chemistry and a set of
properties attractive for potential applications, but also demonstrated a
feasibility of surmounting conceptions common in material sciences.

The relic density of a cold dark matter (CDM) candidate is calculated in the
context of three non-standard cosmological scenaria and its value is compared
with the one obtained in the standard regime. In particular, we consider the
decoupling of the CDM particle during: (i) A decaying-particle dominated phase
or (ii) a kinetic-energy dominated phase or (iii) the decay of a massive
particle under the complete or partial domination of kination. We use plausible
values (from the viewpoint of supersymmetric models) for the mass and the
thermal averaged cross section times the velocity of the cold relic and we
investigate scenaria of equilibrium and non-equilibrium production. In the case
(i) a low reheat temperature, in the range (1-20) GeV, significantly
facilitates the achievement of an acceptable CDM abundance. On the other hand,
the presence of kination in the case (ii) can lead to an enhancement of the CDM
abundance up to three orders of magnitude. The latter enhancement can be
avoided, in the case (iii). In a such situation, the temperature turns out to
be frozen to a plateau value which is, mostly, lower than about 40 GeV.

We study localization phenomena in two dimensional systems of charged
particles in the presence of a metallic ground plane with a particular focus on
the superconductor-insulator transition. The ground plane introduces a
screening of the long-range Coulomb interaction, and provides a source of
dissipation due to the gapless diffusive electrons. The interplay of these two
effects leads to interesting physical phenomena which are analysed in detail in
this paper. We argue that the generic superconductor-insulator transition of
charged particles in the presence of the ground plane may be controlled by a
{\it fixed line} with variable critical exponents. This is illustrated by an
explicit calculation in an appropriate $\epsilon$ expansion. In contrast, the
universal properties of the superconductor-Mott insulator transition in the
clean limit at commensurate densities are shown to be unmodified by either the
metal or the long-range Coulomb interaction. A similar fixed line can arise in
the presence of a metallic ground plane for quantum Hall plateau transitions.
Implications for experiments on Josephson-junction arrays and quantum Hall
systems are described.

Using the square-root map p-->\sqrt{p} a probability density function p can
be represented as a point of the unit sphere S in the Hilbert space of
square-integrable functions. If the density function depends smoothly on a set
of parameters, the image of the map forms a Riemannian submanifold M in S. The
metric on M induced by the ambient spherical geometry of S is the Fisher
information matrix. Statistical properties of the system modelled by a
parametric density function p can then be expressed in terms of information
geometry. An elementary introduction to information geometry is presented,
followed by a precise geometric characterisation of the family of Gaussian
density functions. When the parametric density function describes the
equilibrium state of a physical system, certain physical characteristics can be
identified with geometric features of the associated information manifold M.
Applying this idea, the properties of vapour-liquid phase transitions are
elucidated in geometrical terms. For an ideal gas, phase transitions are absent
and the geometry of M is flat. In this case, the solutions to the geodesic
equations yield the adiabatic equations of state. For a van der Waals gas, the
associated geometry of M is highly nontrivial. The scalar curvature of M
diverges along the spinodal boundary which envelopes the unphysical region in
the phase diagram. The curvature is thus closely related to the stability of
the system.

We are investigating how to refine the structure of the compactly supported smooth (CSS) regulator within the functional renormalization group. This work focuses on two theoretical frameworks: bosonized two-dimensional Quantum Electrodynamics (QED_2) and three-dimensional O(N=1) scalar field theory, both employing the local potential approximation (LPA). We utilize the principle of minimal sensitivity (PMS) to optimize the CSS regulator, showing that it recovers various existing regulator forms under specific conditions. When analyzing different normalization schemes within the PMS method, we found that the CSS regulator originally proposed by Litim is optimal when using the LPA. However, Litim’s precise regulator introduces a discontinuity in the regulator function.  A CSS regulator, practically identical to Litim’s form but with infinite differentiability, still consistently works with the gradient expansion up to high orders. This smooth, analytical behavior is achieved through a small, yet non-zero, exponential fall-off parameter within the CSS regulator. Therefore, a CSS regulator resembling Litim’s optimized form—but incorporating an exponential factor—demonstrates promising characteristics and may help mitigate scheme dependence issues within the functional renormalization group, particularly given the approximations used in our investigations.

We examine the behavior of the two-point function for a non-interacting, massless scalar field in the discretization of spacetime proposed by H.S. Snyder.  We determined that the propagator is real when considered on the Euclidean side of the complex $p^2$ plane and behaves as $1/p^2$ as $p^2$ approaches zero, regardless of whether the analysis is performed from the Euclidean or Minkowski space. Specifically, the real part of the propagator approaches zero smoothly as $p^2$ increases up to the discretization scale of $1/a^2$, remaining zero for $p^2$ values exceeding this scale. This result aligns with the expectation of terminating single-particle propagation at the ultraviolet edge of the discretization. The imaginary part of the propagator is finite and continuous on the Minkowski side, and decreases slowly towards zero as $p^2$ moves beyond $1/a^2$ and continues to infinity. Furthermore, we suggest that the spectral function for these multiparticle states exhibits saturation as the momentum $p^2$ is investigated just beyond the $1/a^2$ discretization scale.  We then explore potential implications of this spectral function for cosmological scenarios.

Mitral valve repair surgery aims to restore the function of the mitral valve. A key step in this procedure involves attaching a prosthetic ring to the mitral annulus. Examining the sutures used to secure this ring – particularly how they pass through the annulus – can be valuable for evaluating a surgeon’s skill, for precise surgical analysis, and for using augmented reality to position a virtual prosthetic ring model. 

This study introduces a neural network method that identifies the sutures in endoscopic images taken during mitral valve repair. This addresses a challenge of landmark detection with a variable number of landmarks, differing from many existing deep learning approaches. The neural network was trained independently on two separate datasets, both utilizing the same architecture and parameters. Each dataset contains over 1,300 stereo image pairs, totaling more than 60,000 annotated landmark points. The resulting heatmap-based neural network achieved a mean positive predictive value (PPV) of 66.68 ± 4.67% and a mean true positive rate (TPR) of 24.45 ± 5.06% on an intraoperative test dataset.  On a dataset gathered during surgical simulations, the network yielded a mean PPV of 81.50 ± 5.77% and a mean TPR of 61.60 ± 6.11%. Optimal detection accuracy is generally observed when the camera is positioned directly above the mitral valve with adequate lighting.  Additionally, successful suture detection can be obtained from a sideways view of the valve, provided the mitral valve itself is clearly visible.

We present a new method for generating strong secondary flows within microchannels at moderate to high flow rates, allowing for precise control and predictable programming on microfluidic platforms.  The flow patterns, normally symmetrical, become disrupted by the presence of cylindrical pillars placed along the channel. Specifically, the symmetry is broken, causing fluid to be pushed away from the center of the channel. To maintain mass conservation, the fluid then moves laterally towards the channel walls. This creates a net secondary flow, visually demonstrated in the accompanying video.

The core design consists of a straight microchannel with posts – cylindrical pillars, although other pillar shapes could also be used – arranged alongside the channel.  The channel dimensions were 200 μm by 50 μm, with pillars having a diameter of 100 μm. Varying the pillar positions within the channel's cross-section allows for tailoring different secondary flow patterns. The longitudinal spacing between the pillars was 600 μm for this particular experiment. The device is effective over a broad flow rate range, with a flow rate of 150 μL/min utilized in this study.  Three inlets are incorporated to deliver a dye stream co-flowing with two water streams. The video illustrates how the net secondary flow, generated by inertial forces, visibly alters the fluorescently labeled dye stream’s cross-sectional appearance.  Consecutive confocal images were captured at the inlet and after every 49 pillars to capture this effect.

We’ve utilized a new method recently created to derive the diagonal form of any quantum Hamiltonian, and applied it specifically to Bloch electrons experiencing an external electromagnetic field. Our analysis yields a series expansion to the second order in $\hbar$ for this diagonal representation. This result is essential for calculating the effective in-band Hamiltonian when considering interacting Bloch electrons in various energy bands. Specifically, accurately describing interactions between magnetic moments, which arise from the magnetic component of the electromagnetic interaction, demands a calculation extending to second order in $\hbar$. We discovered that the electronic current is comprised of two components: one originates from the electron's velocity, and the other is a magnetic moment current—analogous to a spin current for Dirac particles. This magnetic moment current drives interactions between magnetic moments, functioning in a similar way to the Breit Hamiltonian’s spin-spin interaction for Dirac electrons.

Researchers frequently employ molecular line observations using several different tracers to study the motion within molecular clouds. Yet, measurements of cloud velocity dispersions obtained with various lines, even within the same area, often produce conflicting results. The precise causes of these discrepancies aren’t fully understood, largely because molecular line observations are influenced by several potential biases. In this work, we examine and resolve the factors contributing to these linewidth measurement inconsistencies by creating simulated position-position-velocity cubes from a set of computer simulations of magnetohydrodynamic molecular clouds. We compare the linewidths derived from these synthetic observations to the actual values obtained in the simulations. Our analysis reveals that discrepancies between different tracers are due to a combination of effects: density-dependent excitation, where tracers sensitive to higher densities probe smaller areas with lower velocity dispersions; opacity broadening, particularly prominent for tracers like CO that are highly optically thick; and the limitations of resolution and sensitivity, which reduce the intensity of the line's outer regions. We determined that, when considering a consistent signal-to-noise ratio, the J=4->3 line of CO, the J=1->0 line of C<sup>18</sup>O, and the (1,1) inversion transition of NH<sub>3</sub> generally provide the most reliable estimates of velocity dispersion. These tracers accurately reflect the true kinematics of the cloud with an approximate 10% uncertainty, irrespective of the magnetic field strength, the cloud’s evolutionary stage, or the orientation of the line of sight relative to the magnetic field.

Valued constraint satisfaction problems (VCSPs) are discrete optimization problems characterized by an objective function that can take on values from $\mathbb{Q} \cup \{\infty\}$ and is defined as the sum of fixed-arity functions. Boolean surjective VCSPs are a specific type where variables can only assume labels from the set $D = \{0, 1\}$, and an optimal solution requires utilizing both 0 and 1 labels.  Examples of such problems include the classical global Min-Cut problem in graphs and the Minimum Distance problem in coding theory.

We present a dichotomy theorem, providing a comprehensive classification of the complexity of Boolean surjective VCSPs regarding exact solvability. This result extends a previous dichotomy established by Creignou and H\'ebrard for $\{0,\infty\}$-valued constraint languages (equivalent to surjective decision CSPs).  Furthermore, we demonstrate a dichotomy theorem for the maximization problem of $\mathbb{Q}_{\geq 0}$-valued surjective VCSPs concerning approximability.

Interestingly, unlike the situation for regular Boolean surjective (decision) CSPs, we’ve uncovered a new and surprisingly tractable language. This language possesses a notable mathematical structure linked to downsets and upsets. Our main contribution lies in identifying this new class and proving that it resides at the threshold of tractability.  A critical element of our proof involves a polynomial-time algorithm designed to enumerate all near-optimal solutions to a generalized Min-Cut problem, a result that may have independent value.

We investigated the electric dipole (EDM) and Schiff (SFM) moments of the deuteron, induced by interactions with three-pseudoscalar mesons – specifically pions, kaons, eta, and eta’ – using a phenomenological Lagrangian approach.  This approach considers the deuteron as a bound state of a proton and neutron, with its properties determined by both one- and two-body forces. The contribution of the one-body forces, reflecting the proton and neutron as quasi-free constituents, is simply the sum of the individual proton and neutron EDMs. The two-body forces, arising from one-meson exchange between the nucleons, are approximated using a potential model and contribute to the deuteron EDM.  Through careful numerical analysis of nucleon and deuteron EDMs, we established tight constraints on the strengths of CP-violating hadronic couplings and the parameter $\bar\theta$.  Our results demonstrate that future measurements of proton and deuteron EDMs at a level of approximately $10^{-29}$, planned by the Store Ring EDM and JEDI Collaborations, will significantly tighten the existing upper limits on these CP-violating parameters.

Calculating the trace of integer powers of the local density matrix for the vacuum state within a specific region V can be done by evaluating a functional integral over a curved manifold containing conical singularities. While recent work has successfully computed similar integrals for free fields, determining the geometric entropy associated with this process often requires an analytic continuation in terms of the conical angle—a challenging task. Here, we explicitly find this analytic continuation by leveraging a connection between the functional integral formulas and Chung-Peschel expressions describing the density matrix using correlators.  Our result shows that the entropy is expressed as a functional integral in flat Euclidean space, with a restriction applied to the region V, and a particular boundary condition. As an illustration, we determine the exact entanglement entropies for both massive scalar and Dirac free fields in one spatial dimension and one time dimension. This is achieved through solutions to a nonlinear differential equation of the Painlevé V type.

This research aims to understand the source of the observed variability in the connection between the size of the broad line region (R) and the luminosity of active galactic nuclei (AGN).  This relationship is crucial for estimating the masses of black holes in very distant galaxies and may also be useful for determining cosmic distances. Our work focuses on six nearby AGN that have been monitored simultaneously in both ultraviolet and optical wavelengths. We also analyzed the long-term optical brightness changes of the Seyfert 1 galaxy NGC 5548 and used Monte Carlo simulations to assess how the natural variations within individual AGN influence the scatter in this relationship for a larger sample of approximately 40 AGN.  We discovered that the scatter in this relationship is linked to the color of the AGN.  For individual AGN, the size of the Hβ-emitting region is more strongly correlated with the nuclear optical luminosity than with ultraviolet luminosity, which contributes roughly 0.08 dex of scatter to the overall relationship. This results from a non-linear connection between changes in the ionizing continuum and those in the optical continuum.  Furthermore, our study emphasizes how important it is to account for and minimize this scatter—it directly impacts the measured relationship.  We found that replacing the ultraviolet luminosity with the ionizing luminosity could significantly decrease the current observed scatter, reducing it by approximately 0.13 dex.

Dealing with the intricacies of language and vision presents significant challenges in artificial intelligence. While language can offer a useful initial understanding, leading to seemingly accurate answers without true comprehension of the visual content, this can slow advancements in computer vision within multi-modal systems. To tackle this, we focused on binary Visual Question Answering (VQA) using abstract scenes. We framed the task as a visual verification problem, representing each question as a concise tuple—essentially a summary of the visual concept being sought in an image. If that concept is present, the answer is “yes”; otherwise, it's “no.” Abstract scenes are valuable because they encourage a focus on the overall meaning of the question and, crucially, allow us to carefully control the influence of language prior knowledge.  We created a dataset by pairing identical questions with specific images where the answer is “yes” or “no,” emphasizing that language alone wasn’t sufficient for optimal performance on our balanced dataset. Our method achieved performance comparable to a current state-of-the-art VQA system when using the standard, unbalanced dataset, and demonstrated superior results when using the carefully balanced dataset we developed.

Cosmological N-body simulations were used to examine the distribution of velocities within dark matter haloes, specifically focusing on the even moments of the distribution – dispersion and kurtosis. We analyzed ten massive haloes within the simulation volume, modeling their density using the NFW formula and characterizing them by their virial mass and concentration.  We also calculated the velocity anisotropy parameter for these haloes, observing that it was moderately radial and increased with distance from the halo’s center.  The radial velocity dispersion displayed a peak profile, while the radial kurtosis decreased from a near-Gaussian value closer to the center. These results support the conclusion that dark matter haloes have velocity distributions that are intrinsically non-Gaussian and exhibit a flattened top. Our calculated velocity moments for the simulated haloes closely matched the predictions of Jeans equations, using the anisotropy values determined from the simulations. We also investigated the velocity moments of a composite cluster composed of these ten haloes extending out to ten virial radii.  In this outer region, the velocity dispersion decreased consistently, approaching the background value, while the kurtosis rose from below to above 3, indicating a shift from a flat-topped to a strongly peaked distribution. This behavior suggests the presence of ordered flow with low dispersion.  We illustrated this transition by visualizing the velocity distribution in various radial bins within the composite cluster.

We introduce a new topology optimization framework designed to create macroscopic bodies with precisely controlled elastic behavior. This framework leverages a spatially-variant cellular structure at the microscale to achieve this optimization. The underlying cellular network, inspired by natural microstructures formed during spinodal decomposition, allows for smooth variations in properties and tunable anisotropy, and is defined by a limited number of design parameters linked to a Gaussian random field.  We discretize the overall problem using finite elements, and these elements continuously interpolate the microscale design parameters alongside the displacement field.  Assuming a separation of scales, we represent the macroscale’s material behavior as the homogenized elastic response of the microstructure, determined by these local design parameters.  Unlike traditional methods, we avoid computationally intensive microscale homogenization by employing a data-driven approach using deep neural networks. This model accurately predicts the effective elasticity tensor based on the microscale design parameters. The network is trained using stiffness data obtained through numerical finite element homogenization.  Furthermore, this machine learning setup provides automatic differentiation, enabling the exact computation of sensitivities—a crucial element for optimization—without relying on numerical approximations.  This capability extends beyond elasticity, suggesting this framework offers a novel path for multiscale topology optimization utilizing surrogate models.

This project aims to achieve the highest score in the Axelrod Tournament. Our initial approach involved analyzing cooperation rates from the top strategies within the Axelrod Library to design a Finite State Machine (FSM). A first ten-state FSM, based on these cooperation rates, performed well, ranking within the top 35 of 220 strategies in the short run time category.  Following a brief evolutionary algorithm over 50 generations, this original ten-state FSM was refined into an eight-state FSM, which then placed among the top five in the short run time category. Subsequently, this eight-state FSM underwent a more extensive evolutionary process, continuing for 500 generations. This resulted in another eight-state FSM, which achieved first place within the entire Axelrod Library – including both short and long run time strategies.  Finally, this final eight-state FSM was reduced to a six-state FSM, allowing it to secure first place in the full Axelrod Tournament, outperforming all other strategies, whether run quickly or over a longer period.

This work reports the first lattice QCD calculation of pion transverse-momentum-dependent wave functions (TMDWFs). We performed numerical simulations using two different lattice ensembles: one employing 2+1+1 flavors of highly improved staggered quarks with a lattice spacing of $a = 0.121$ fm, generated by the MILC Collaboration, and another utilizing 2+1 flavors of clover fermions and a tree-level Symanzik gauge action at $a = 0.098$ fm, provided by the CLS Collaboration. A crucial step involved obtaining the soft function, achieved by including one-loop perturbative corrections and ensuring proper normalization. Using this soft function, along with equal-time quasi-TMDWFs computed on the lattice, we derived the light-cone TMDWFs. The results from the two lattice ensembles were found to be consistent, and a comparison with existing phenomenological models was conducted. These investigations represent an initial $ab$ $initio$ calculation of TMDWFs, aiming to ultimately furnish essential theoretical inputs for predicting outcomes in exclusive QCD processes through factorization techniques.

Granular media moving at high speeds can often be effectively represented as a gas composed of hard spheres colliding inelastically. At moderate densities, the Enskog kinetic equation, modified to account for these inelastic collisions, forms a key foundation for understanding granular hydrodynamics. However, a notable observation, unlike what’s seen in typical molecular gases, is that energy isn’t equally distributed among the different components in granular mixtures, even when they’re in a homogeneous state. Specifically, each component has a partial temperature ($T_i$) reflecting its average kinetic energy, which differs from the total granular temperature ($T$). This paper aims to survey how these various partial temperatures influence the mixture's transport properties. Initially, we examine how the unequal distribution of energy impacts transport, a consequence of the inelastic collisions—a deviation not present when collisions are perfectly elastic. We demonstrate that this effect is particularly significant in processes like thermal diffusion segregation. Furthermore, we investigate a separate source of energy nonequipartition stemming from the flow velocity diverging. This effect, previously examined in earlier studies of dense molecular mixtures, influences the bulk viscosity coefficient.  We compare our analytical results (which are approximations) with those obtained from Monte Carlo and molecular dynamics simulations, confirming that kinetic theory is a reliable method for describing the behavior of granular flows.

Consider a finite set of points, X, in R^n. A nonnegative polynomial on X can be expressed as the sum of squares of rational functions, up to the vanishing ideal I(X). In applications like polynomial optimization, we’re particularly interested in representations using rational functions of low degree. We establish a general upper bound for these representations, expressed in terms of the Hilbert function of X. We demonstrate that this upper bound is sharp for quadratic functions defined on the hypercube C = {0,1}^n—a case that has been extensively analyzed in combinatorial optimization. Based on the lower bounds obtained for the hypercube, we construct a family of globally nonnegative quartic polynomials that cannot be represented as sums of squares of rational functions with a low degree. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first construction for Hilbert’s 17th problem offering a family of polynomials with a bounded degree, requiring increasingly higher degrees in rational function representations as the number of variables, n, increases towards infinity. The study of representation theory involving the symmetric group S_n plays a significant role in proving our lower bounds.

We build upon previous work (Watarai 2006) that developed analytic formulas for optically-thick supercritical accretion flows. In this paper, we present similar formulas for optically-thin, one-temperature accretion flows, incorporating the advection-dominated regime. We utilize the “semi-iterative” method outlined in our earlier paper. These new formulas yield two distinct, real solutions. The first represents the advection-dominated accretion flow (ADAF), while the second corresponds to the radiation-dominated accretion flow, often referred to as the Shapiro, Lightman, & Eardley (SLE) model. Both solutions are determined by a cubic equation relating to the advection parameter, *f*, which represents the ratio of the advection cooling rate ($Q_{\rm adv}$) to the viscous heating rate ($Q_{\rm vis}$): *f* = *Q<sub>adv</sub>*/ *Q<sub>vis</sub>*. While prior studies typically assumed *f* to be constant (often around 1 for ADAF), we demonstrate that it’s more accurately a function of the radiative cooling parameters. Specifically, we found that *f* can be expressed as a function of the radius, mass accretion rate, and the viscous parameter α. This allows us to estimate the radius at which the optically-thin ADAF transitions to the outer, optically-thick standard disk. Observing the quiescent state in black hole X-ray binaries can potentially be used to measure this transition radius.

Researchers have extensively studied the coordinated patterns of oscillations and the changes in the X-ray spectrum observed during thermonuclear X-ray bursts in neutron-star X-ray binaries, often examining these phenomena independently. Using data from the Rossi X-ray Timing Explorer, we analyzed all the X-ray bursts from the source 4U 1728-34. Our analysis revealed that the appearance of burst oscillations can be used to anticipate how the blackbody radius evolves during the cooling phase. Bursts that exhibit oscillations maintain a relatively stable blackbody radius for approximately 2 to 8 seconds during this cooling period. In contrast, bursts without oscillations either maintain a constant radius for over 2 to 8 seconds or experience a rapid, fluctuating decrease and increase of the radius – faster than 2 seconds. The observed oscillations and the evolving spectral behavior of the bursts are both influenced by the accretion rate. Furthermore, we observed differences in the light curves of the bursts: bursts with oscillations have a short rise time, around 0.5 seconds, and display both positive and negative convexity. Conversely, bursts lacking oscillations have a longer rise time, approximately 1 second, and primarily exhibit positive convexity. This pattern aligns with the suggestion that burst oscillations are linked to ignition occurring off the neutron star’s equator.

Quantum dots offer a valuable research platform for studying quantum thermoelectricity due to their controllable electronic properties and the way they exhibit quantum confinement. Remarkably, the nonlinear thermoelectric characteristics of these tiny systems can impact the effectiveness of thermoelectric power conversion. In this work, we measured both thermovoltage and thermocurrent in quantum dots integrated within semiconductor nanowires. These measurements revealed a strong nonlinearity in response to the applied thermal bias. Our analysis indicates that most of the observed nonlinear behavior arises from a modification of the quantum dot’s energy levels—a process we termed renormalization—that changes with the applied thermal bias. We developed a theoretical model incorporating renormalization to explain the nonlinear thermovoltage. Additionally, we suggest that a pronounced renormalization effect might be caused by the melting of Kondo correlations, particularly in materials experiencing a mixed-valence regime. This control over nonlinear thermoelectric effects has significant potential for improving the efficiency of thermoelectric energy conversion.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans often produce relatively consistent results because of the brain's inherent structure, and because the data is further processed to align with a standardized template through techniques like linear and non-linear transformations. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs), however, are typically built to handle data with significant variations, such as natural images, by using convolutional filters that move across an image.  In this work, we propose a novel CNN architecture that merges the concept of hierarchical feature learning in neural networks with an understanding of the spatial uniformity of MRI data. Specifically, the initial layers of our network are trained using traditional convolutional layers globally. For the higher, more abstract layers, we implement Patch Individual Filters (PIF). PIF layers learn features by focusing on specific image regions—patches—without sharing weights between them. This approach allows for faster learning and improved performance, particularly when training data is limited. We tested PIF layers across three different datasets and tasks: sex classification using data from the UK Biobank, Alzheimer’s disease detection with the ADNI dataset, and multiple sclerosis detection utilizing private hospital data. Our results indicate that CNNs incorporating PIF layers achieve higher accuracies, particularly in situations with limited data, and require fewer training epochs to reach convergence. To our knowledge, this is the first study to explicitly incorporate a prior related to the spatial properties of brain MRI when training CNNs.

We investigated the potential signatures of Majorana fermions in a novel device consisting of a topological superconductor-quantum dot-topological superconductor hybrid. This device was fabricated using an epitaxially grown indium antimonide (InSb) nanowire with two niobium (Nb) superconducting contacts on a silicon/silicon dioxide (Si/SiO2) substrate. At low temperatures, a quantum dot formed within a portion of the InSb nanowire between the Nb contacts. The two InSb nanowire segments contacted by the Nb contacts also exhibited superconductivity through the proximity effect. Measurements of charge transport revealed clear Coulomb diamonds and evidence of the Kondo effect within the quantum dot’s Coulomb blockade regime at zero magnetic field.  When a sufficiently strong magnetic field was applied, a zero-bias conductance peak appeared in the Coulomb blockade regime. Notably, this conductance peak consistently appeared across multiple adjacent Coulomb diamonds, regardless of the quantum dot’s particle occupancy. Furthermore, the zero-bias conductance peak was frequently accompanied by two additional conductance peaks, creating a distinctive triple-peak pattern.  The distance between these side peaks varied in a manner closely linked to the background Coulomb conductance oscillations of the device. These observations are consistent with the expected behavior of Majorana fermions in a nanowire based topological superconductor-quantum dot-topological superconductor system, where two Majorana bound states near the quantum dot pair up to form quasi-particle states with discernible energies, and the remaining two Majorana bound states remain as zero-energy modes at the ends of the InSb nanowire.

Schur-Weyl duality, initially investigated through the simultaneous actions of the symmetric group $S_d$ and $\mathrm{GL}(n,\mathbb{C})$ on $V^{\otimes d}$, where $V = \mathbb{C}^n$, was broadened by Drinfeld and Jimbo to include the finite Iwahori-Hecke algebra $H_d(q^2)$ and quantum algebras $U_q(\mathrm{gl}(n))$ using universal $R$-matrices that satisfy the Yang-Baxter equation.  This framework expanded with two further developments: Chari and Pressley’s extension to the affine case, and Moon and Mitsuhashi’s work in the super case.  We continue this line of research by examining the general affine super case, specifically relating the commuting actions of the affine Iwahori-Hecke algebra $H^a_d(q^2)$ and the affine quantum Lie superalgebra $U_{q,a}^\sigma(\mathrm{sl}(m,n))$.  This is done using the presentation of Yamane, which relies on generators and relations, and operates on the $d$th tensor product of the superspace $V = \mathbb{C}^{m+n}$.  As a result, we build a functor demonstrating that it establishes an equivalence between the categories of $H_d^a(q^2)$ and $U_{q,a}^\sigma(\mathrm{sl}(m,n))$ modules when $d < m+n$.

This paper introduces a new method using 3D convolutional neural networks (ConvNets) to automatically detect pulmonary nodules in low-dose CT scans. This is an important area of research for early lung cancer diagnosis and treatment due to the inherent difficulty of the task. Unlike traditional ConvNet approaches, our work addresses the common problem of significant imbalances in medical datasets—where some samples are much easier to classify than others—by leveraging localized annotations to improve the network’s learning process and, ultimately, its detection accuracy.  Our framework is organized into two main stages: initially, we employ a 3D fully convolutional network trained with an online sample filtering technique to quickly and accurately identify potential nodule locations. Subsequently, we utilize a hybrid-loss residual network that incorporates both the location and size of the nodules as key indicators for precise recognition. Evaluation on the publicly available LUNA16 dataset shows that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art techniques for pulmonary nodule detection.

For over four decades, scientists have been trying to determine the cause of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs), incredibly powerful and brief bursts of gamma radiation lasting from less than a second to several minutes.  There’s still a significant amount of uncertainty surrounding these events, largely because of unanswered questions about their physical processes. These include what the jets are made of, how they lose energy, how particles within them accelerate, and how radiation is produced. Recent observations of the initial, or “prompt,” emission using the Fermi telescope have intensified these debates and spurred considerable theoretical research, exploring a range of potential explanations. I will examine these debates and suggest a prevailing view based on the current data: Some GRBs exhibit a relatively warm, thermal component in their spectra, thought to be coming from the photosphere of the material ejected at near-light speed. While this thermal component can sometimes be the most prominent feature in a GRB’s spectrum, as seen in bursts like GRB 090902B, it’s typically a smaller “shoulder” in the overall spectrum or undetectable altogether.  The dominant portion of the spectrum, known as the “Band” component, most likely results from synchrotron radiation in the region where the emission is optically thin.  It is probably the variability in the magnetic field within the GRB's central engine that accounts for the diversity of properties observed across different bursts.

The Tunka Radio Extension (Tunka-Rex) is a digital antenna array designed to study radio emissions generated by air showers created by ultra-high energy cosmic rays. It operates within the 30-80 MHz frequency band and is situated alongside the TAIGA experiment in Siberia.  Tunka-Rex utilizes 63 antennas, with 57 of them deployed over a 1 square kilometer area that’s densely equipped with instrumentation. The radio signals from these air showers are brief pulses, lasting tens of nanoseconds, and are recorded on traces that are approximately 5 microseconds long.  The team analyzes cosmic-ray events by reconstructing these signals, primarily by determining their precise positions within the traces and their amplitudes. However, at low signal-to-noise levels—when the traces are largely obscured by background noise—this reconstruction can be challenging. To improve detection sensitivity and overall efficiency, the researchers employ advanced signal reconstruction techniques, specifically matched filtering and deep neural networks using an autoencoder architecture. This paper presents a comparison of signal reconstructions produced using these methods and provides an initial example of a reconstruction performed with a deep neural network for Tunka-Rex signals.

Recent experiments demonstrating spin-orbit coupling in ultracold atomic gases have created a valuable tool for investigating various quantum effects. In these experiments, “spin” refers to the intrinsic angular momentum of the atoms (either spin-1/2 or spin-1), while “orbit” describes their linear momentum. We suggest a method to achieve a novel type of spin-tensor–momentum coupling (STMC) within spin-1 ultracold atomic gases. We examine the ground state behavior of interacting Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs) incorporating STMC and observe unexpected stripe superfluid phases and multicritical points during phase transitions. Notably, STMC enables the study of quantum states exhibiting dynamic stripe orders – patterns of density modulation with a long, adjustable period and high visibility. This discovery suggests a potential route to directly observe a new dynamical supersolid-like state. This STMC generation technique is adaptable to different systems and has the potential to unlock new insights into quantum physics and applications in device design.

Point cloud registration can be difficult due to the presence of noise and the sheer volume of points involved, which complicates finding accurate correspondences. Because current approaches largely depend on this search process, it remains an active area of research. To address this issue, we introduce a new data-driven registration algorithm that utilizes deep generative neural networks. Our goal is to directly generate aligned point cloud pairs, a valuable capability for applications such as 3D object matching and retrieval. We designed an end-to-end generative neural network – incorporating three novel elements – to achieve this. First, we created a PointMixer network, utilizing a point multi-perception layer (MLP) mixer to effectively capture both global and local details from the point cloud itself at various scales. Second, we developed a feature interaction module to combine information between the two point clouds being registered. Finally, a parallel and differential sample consensus method was developed to determine the transformation matrix, based on the generated alignment. The neural network is trained within a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) framework, preserving both the data’s overall distribution and structural similarities. Testing on the ModelNet40 and 7Scene datasets shows that our method surpasses existing techniques in accuracy and speed. Specifically, it reduces registration error (CD) by a factor of two and decreases computation time by twelve times compared to traditional correspondence-based algorithms.

We begin by exploring the basis for using a renormalization group (RG) analysis to study a system undergoing dimensional reduction due to strong magnetic fields. We highlight the crucial observation that the low-energy excitations always possess a marginal behavior, regardless of the coupling constant within the initial theoretical framework. Subsequently, we develop a scale-dependent effective four-Fermi interaction, arising from the screening of photons at weak coupling. We then demonstrate the proper application of the RG method, accounting for this screening effect, where the evolution from ultraviolet to infrared scales is divided into two stages defined by the screening mass scale.  Comparing the dynamical mass gaps calculated from both the RG equations and Schwinger-Dyson equations, we find a strong agreement between them. Specifically, applying this to Quantum Electrodynamics (QED) and the Nambu–Jona-Lasinio model, we examine how interaction properties influence the mass gap and emphasize the need to consider inherent energy scale dependencies within the original theories when determining the mass gap. These results are anticipated to contribute to understanding magnetic catalysis in Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD).

Monte Carlo simulations have traditionally been able to accommodate increasingly complex models, expanding to infinite dimensions. However, this flexibility relies on a key constraint: a state variable can only be expressed as a function of other state variables if that function is linear. This work demonstrates a way to overcome this limitation by representing nonlinearities using polynomial approximations and expanding the problem’s dimensionality. We demonstrate the viability of this approach with several practical examples, including modeling phytoplankton growth in light-limited conditions, radiative transfer in planetary atmospheres, electromagnetic scattering by particles, and solar power plant production – scenarios previously challenging for Monte Carlo methods. We also highlight an advantage of the method when dealing with problems involving many interacting particles, noting that it simplifies the handling of rare events. The extension retains the characteristics that made Monte Carlo widely used, specifically maintaining the ability to refine models and manage complexity without impacting convergence rates, regardless of the added dimensions.

Dauchet et al. (2018) developed this technique. *Scientific Reports*, *8*, 13302, DOI: 10.1038/s41598-018-31574-4

Supernova neutrinos possess unique properties that can produce significant physical effects. Initially, their wave packets are incredibly narrow, with a size of approximately $10^{-11}$ cm—resulting in a substantial energy uncertainty that’s comparable to the neutrino’s energy itself, and a short coherence length. As they travel to Earth, these wave packets representing different neutrino masses expand dramatically and begin to separate. Once within the Earth, these mass eigenstates further split into eigenstates reflecting the influence of matter, and subsequently oscillate. The coherence length inside the Earth is roughly equal to the Earth’s radius.  This research examines these characteristics and their implications. Specifically, we: (i) provide refined estimates of the wave packet size; (ii) investigate the decoherence condition for spread-out wave packets, demonstrating that it remains unchanged by their expansion; and (iii) analyze the coherence of neutrinos traversing a layered medium with abrupt density changes between layers.  In such situations, a “catch-up effect” can partially restore coherence, extending the coherence length beyond typical predictions.  This phenomenon could occur for supernova neutrinos as they pass through shock wave fronts in the star’s explosion or within the Earth's core.

Visual perception for robots relies on accurately estimating geometric features from images, including depth, camera motion, and optical flow. This work introduces a joint self-supervised method to estimate these three elements simultaneously. The depth network, optical flow network, and camera motion network are designed to operate independently yet are trained together during the learning process. This joint training approach leverages the inherent relationships between the elements, utilizing both dynamic and static scene information more effectively than independent training methods.

We have enhanced this joint self-supervision technique through three key improvements: modifications to the network architecture, a dynamic object segmentation strategy, and the incorporation of geometric constraints. Specifically, we added an attention mechanism to the camera motion network to better capture similarities in camera movement between frames, utilizing a novel plug-and-play convolutional attention module inspired by the Transformer architecture. To address dynamic objects, we developed a thresholding algorithm to identify and mask these regions within the loss function, recognizing their different impacts on both the optical flow and depth-pose self-supervised frameworks. Furthermore, we employ traditional methods to estimate the fundamental matrix from corresponding points, applying this estimation as a constraint for the camera motion network. 

We evaluated our method using the KITTI dataset. Results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in estimating pose and optical flow, alongside competitive depth estimation results compared to other joint self-supervised techniques. The code for this work is available at https://github.com/jianfenglihg/Unsupervised_geometry.

Trapped ultracold polar molecules on an optical lattice form a complex many-body system capable of exhibiting collective excitations similar to excitons found in solid crystals. This work focuses on rotational excitations of these molecules, specifically rotational Frenkel excitons. No naturally occurring material besides solid hydrogen displays this phenomenon. These rotational excitons possess distinctive characteristics that allow us to adjust non-linear exciton interactions and exciton-impurity scattering by using an external electric field. We demonstrate how this approach can investigate the contrasting roles of dynamic and kinematic exciton-exciton interactions in excitonic energy transfer, and to investigate quantum localization within a dynamically controlled disordered potential. Furthermore, these rotational excitons provide a platform for simulating condensed matter models that are difficult to achieve with ultracold atoms. As an illustration, we explore using polar molecules on an optical lattice to model the Holstein model.

This research introduces a novel probability distribution, the inverse xgamma distribution (IXGD). We examine several key mathematical and statistical properties of the IXGD, including its reliability characteristics, moments, inverse moments, stochastic ordering, and order statistics.  We explored various methods for estimating the distribution's parameters, specifically the maximum likelihood method, the least squares method, the weighted least squares method, the Cramér-von Mises method, and the maximum product spacing method.  Furthermore, we calculated an asymptotic confidence interval (ACI) for each parameter estimate.  To evaluate these estimators, we conducted a simulation study, comparing their average widths and coverage probabilities. Finally, we demonstrated the practical applicability of the IXGD by analyzing two real-world datasets.

Large, destructive earthquakes occurring beneath tectonic plates – known as megathrust earthquakes – pose a serious threat to human populations.  A particularly dangerous megathrust earthquake is anticipated to strike the Nankai Trough off the coast of southwestern Japan, an area characterized by economic activity and a high concentration of people, and with a history of these types of events. These earthquakes result from the process of one tectonic plate sliding beneath another, typically at locations with a ‘slip-deficit’ or ‘coupling’ zone along the boundary.  Researchers have long sought to understand the distribution of these slip-deficits to better forecast potential earthquakes. However, previous studies were limited by a lack of detailed seafloor measurements.

The Hydrographic and Oceanographic Department of the Japan Coast Guard has been working to establish a sophisticated and lasting network of seafloor geodetic observations within this subduction zone to gain direct insight into the offshore slip-deficit rates.  We now share data from this network and a model depicting the offshore distribution of interplate slip-deficits.  Our findings reveal that most offshore areas within the subduction zone exhibit positive slip-deficits.  Notably, we identified previously unrecognized areas of high slip-deficit, which are critical for tsunami preparedness. Conversely, we also found regions with lower-than-expected slip-deficit rates, aligning with the presence of shallow, slow earthquakes and submerged volcanic seamounts.  This represents the first direct link between coupling conditions and these interconnected seismic and geological processes. These observations offer essential new data for developing earthquake scenarios and further research into the dynamics of the Nankai Trough subduction zone.

This paper introduces ADDA-KR, an adaptive deep density approximation strategy designed for solving steady-state Fokker-Planck (F-P) equations. F-P equations frequently involve many dimensions and are defined over unbounded domains, making traditional grid-based numerical methods challenging to apply. We propose a novel flow-based generative model called KRnet, utilizing the Knothe-Rosenblatt rearrangement to create a family of probability density functions—effective candidates for approximating the F-P equations. KRnet’s approach exhibits a weaker dependence on dimensionality compared to conventional computation and efficiently estimates high-dimensional density functions. To determine appropriate points for approximating the equation, we employ an adaptive sampling procedure, iteratively generating samples based on the density function calculated at each step. We outline a comprehensive ADDA-KR framework, and confirm its accuracy and efficiency through extensive numerical experiments.

Recent decisions by the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) allow secondary users (SUs) to utilize TV white spaces (TVWS) once they have access to a geographic database listing vacant television channels. Consequently, sustainable business models are crucial for companies operating these databases. Database access is currently available under two distinct pricing structures: the registration scheme and the service plan scheme. The registration scheme allocates a portion of TV bandwidth to registered White Space Devices (WSDs), while the service plan scheme charges WSDs based on their data queries. This paper examines a business model for the TVWS database using a combined pricing approach. We explore a situation where a database operator uses both schemes to cater to SUs.  The choices of SUs regarding which pricing scheme to utilize are modeled as a non-cooperative game, and we establish algorithms that lead to a Nash Equilibrium (NE).  By analyzing the NE of these SUs' choices, the database operator can determine the optimal pricing levels for both schemes—specifically, the amount of bandwidth reserved, the registration fees charged, and the details of the query plans.

Researchers have fabricated and assembled a hybrid single-cavity linac as a proof-of-concept injector for a cancer therapy synchrotron. This design combines a radio frequency quadrupole (RFQ) structure and a drift tube (DT) structure into a single interdigital-H (IH) cavity.  The system utilizes a direct plasma injection scheme (DPIS) for injecting the beam, as this is currently the only viable method for accelerating intense current heavy ions produced by a laser ion source (LIS).  The target input beam current for the HSC linac was specified at 20 milliamperes (mA) of C6+ ions.  Through numerical simulations, the linac was shown capable of accelerating a 6-mA C6+ beam, fulfilling the particle number requirement (approximately 10<sup>8</sup> to 9 ions per pulse) needed for cancer therapy applications.  Employing the HSC injector with DPIS eliminates the need for traditional multi-turn injection systems and the stripping system, and also allows for a smaller beam pipe within the existing synchrotron magnets.  This downsizing is expected to significantly reduce the overall cost of the synchrotron—potentially saving millions of dollars. This paper details the measurements and evaluations performed on the assembled HSC linac, as well as the results of high-power tests conducted using a LIS in conjunction with the DPIS method.

This paper examines various approaches to using deep learning—specifically artificial neural networks—for creating musical content. Our analysis is structured around five key dimensions: Objective, Representation, Architecture, Challenge, and Strategy. 

First, we consider the Objective: what kind of musical content is being generated – for instance, a melody, polyphony, or accompaniment? And for what purpose is it intended? Will it be performed by a human musician (such as in a musical score) or by a machine producing an audio file?

Next, we explore the Representation – specifically, what musical elements are being manipulated. This includes options like waveforms, spectrograms, notes, chords, meter, and beats. We also investigate the format used for these representations, considering options like MIDI, piano rolls, or text-based formats, and how they are encoded—for example, through scalar, one-hot, or many-hot encodings.

The Architecture dimension focuses on the specific deep neural network models employed. This encompasses types such as feedforward networks, recurrent networks, autoencoders, and generative adversarial networks.

We also address the Challenges – outlining the limitations and unanswered questions within this field, such as ensuring variability, interactivity, and genuine creativity in the generated music.

Finally, our Strategy looks at how the music generation process is modeled and controlled, including methods like single-step feedforward approaches, iterative processes, sampling techniques, and techniques that allow for direct manipulation of the input. 

For each dimension, we perform a comparative analysis of existing models and techniques, ultimately proposing a tentative multidimensional typology. This typology is developed through a bottom-up approach, analyzing a diverse selection of existing deep-learning music generation systems identified through literature review. These systems are detailed and used to illustrate the range of choices made regarding objective, representation, architecture, challenge, and strategy, concluding with a discussion of future directions.

Autoregressive language models (ALMs) trained with maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) are commonly used for generating text without any initial prompts. However, these generated texts often exhibit low quality and lack diversity, a problem known as exposure bias—a statistical difference between the real text and what’s produced. Studies have shown that a discriminator can identify this bias. Because the discriminator captures more information than the generator, it has the potential to improve the generator’s performance. Traditional generative adversarial networks (GANs) attempt to address this bias by using the discriminator to directly update the generator, but they often struggle to do so effectively due to differences in the input data. Specifically, the discriminator and the ALM receive different types of input.
