We connect the dynamics within the glassy phase to the depinning transition of Directed Polymers, revealing that changes in parameters like energy can cause abrupt shifts in the preferred pathways and lead to singular behavior in the overlap correlation function between quantum states.  Comparing our results on loop-less Cayley trees and Random Regular Graphs, we explore the impact of loops; specifically, we find that Random Regular Graphs exhibit slow dynamics and apparent power laws over a long timescale, but these are ultimately cut off at a time scale that diverges as the MBL transition is approached.

In half-metallic ferromagnets, spin-polarized Cooper pairs can only form if they exhibit strong, non-local, and time-antisymmetric correlations. This leads to the formation of an odd-frequency superconducting condensate. Using both theoretical analysis and numerical simulations, we show that quantum vortices can arise within these half-metals even without the presence of typical superconducting pairings. Since these metals only conduct through one spin channel, we observe that a circulating spin current accompanies these vortices. Furthermore, we find that magnetic imperfections at the interface with a superconductor affect where vortices tend to form. This finding can aid in determining the effective magnetization alignment angles in hybrid structures, as the location of the vortices is directly measurable using scanning tunneling microscopy (STM). Finally, we provide a discussion of which superconducting order parameter might be appropriate for describing odd-frequency triplet Cooper pairs within a quasiclassical framework.

Researchers compared the detailed longitudinal profile of the highest-energy cosmic-ray air shower ever observed, recorded by the Fly’s Eye detector and with a primary energy of approximately 320 EeV, to simulations generated using the CORSIKA code. These simulations considered both primary photons and different types of primary particles – hadrons – and incorporated detailed modeling of preshower formation in the geomagnetic field when dealing with photons. For hadron primaries, the QGSJET01 and SIBYLL2.1 hadronic interaction models were used. The predicted longitudinal shower profiles were then compared to the observed data. A technique was presented for testing whether a particular primary particle type caused the event, while also accounting for natural fluctuations in the shower. The Fly’s Eye event was consistent with a hadron primary ranging from protons to iron nuclei, regardless of which interaction model (QGSJET01 or SIBYLL2.1) was used. However, the profiles predicted for lighter nuclei differed slightly between the two models. The simulated photon profiles deviated from the observed data by about 1.5 standard deviations. Despite not aligning strongly with the observation, the primary photon hypothesis wasn’t ruled out for this specific event.

As privacy concerns grow, synthetic data generation is becoming increasingly vital. Though autoencoder-based methods are popular for this task, drawing samples from their latent spaces can be difficult. Currently, mixture models offer the most efficient sampling technique for these spaces. In this research, we introduce a new method that represents the latent space of an autoencoder as a simplex, and we’ve developed a unique heuristic to determine the optimal number of components within the mixture model. This heuristic is independent of the number of classes and consistently produces results comparable to others. We’ve also devised a sampling strategy utilizing probability mass functions, leveraging the compactness of the latent space.  We tested our approaches using a synthetic dataset and assessed their effectiveness on three standard datasets: MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CelebA. Our method achieved FID scores of 4.29, 13.55, and 11.90 on these datasets, respectively. These represent significant improvements over existing autoencoder results, with the previous best FID scores for those datasets being 6.3, 85.3, and 35.6. Although autoencoders don’t outperform Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) on CIFAR and CelebA, we achieved a noticeable enhancement (0.21) over the current GAN record for the MNIST dataset. It’s important to note that GANs currently hold the best performance on all three benchmark datasets.

Recent advances in generative models for visual data have enabled the creation of images with remarkable quality and variety. This research investigates the representations learned by a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) generator. Specifically, we demonstrate that these representations can be readily mapped onto semantic segmentation maps using a simple decoder. Importantly, this semantic projection can be learned effectively with only a limited number of labeled images.  Inspired by this result, we introduce the LayerMatch method, which approximates the GAN generator’s representation and can be used for unsupervised pretraining tailored to specific domains. We explore a semi-supervised learning setup where a small amount of labeled data is combined with a larger unlabeled dataset from the same domain.  Our experiments reveal that using a LayerMatch-pretrained backbone yields better accuracy than standard supervised pretraining on ImageNet. Furthermore, this straightforward approach surpasses recent semi-supervised semantic segmentation techniques that leverage both labeled and unlabeled data during training.  The source code required to replicate our experiments will be released alongside the publication.

This work examines the properties of the Lieb lattice, a face-centered square lattice, when subjected to applied gauge fields. We demonstrate that an Abelian gauge field results in a unique quantum Hall effect, stemming from the single Dirac cone and flat bands present in the system’s energy spectrum. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of an intrinsic spin-orbit term – a non-Abelian gauge field – and find that this leads to the quantum spin Hall effect. We also derive the relativistic Hamiltonian that accurately describes the Lieb lattice at low energies and identify the corresponding Landau levels in the presence of both Abelian and non-Abelian gauge fields. Finally, we propose specific methods for creating these gauge fields using cold fermionic atoms trapped in an optical Lieb lattice. Specifically, we detail an efficient way to simulate the Kane-Mele spin-orbit term using assisted tunneling, enabling the creation of various topological states with cold atoms.

Jacobi matrices are a foundational concept in spectral theory, and CMV (Chebyshev-Voloshov) matrices, though more recently developed, are closely linked to a longstanding area of study: orthogonal polynomials on the unit circle – much like Jacobi matrices are connected to polynomials on the real axis. This paper explores a third matrix type within this family. These matrices are generated using orthonormal function systems stemming from the Strong Moment Problem, and we’ve termed them SMP matrices. We will examine, for example, the spectral sets of these periodic SMP matrices.  Analogous to the analysis of Jacobi matrices, the descriptions of SMP matrices are often characterized by conformal mappings applied to hyperbolic domains, specifically comb domains. We can also represent functional models associated with both periodic and almost periodic SMP matrices. We are particularly interested in the potential role these matrices could play in addressing the Killip-Simon problem, concerning Jacobi matrices with an essential spectrum spanning two distinct intervals. The primary outcome of this work is a parametric description of SMP matrices of the Killip-Simon class, defined with an essential spectrum extending across two arbitrary intervals.

To verify if a function $f: \mathbb{F}_q^n\to \mathbb{F}_q$ is a codeword of the Reed-Muller code of dimension $n$ and order $d$ over the finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$ (where $q$ is a prime and $f$ represents the evaluation of a degree-$d$ polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_q$), we employ several multiplication-based tests. Specifically, we consider the following:

*   $\mathrm{Test}_{e,k}$: This test involves selecting $k$ independent random degree-$e$ polynomials, $P_1, \dots, P_k$. It accepts the function if the product $fP_1\cdots P_k$ is the evaluation of a degree-$(d+ek)$ polynomial – effectively, a codeword of the Reed-Muller code with dimension $n$ and order $(d+ek)$.

We demonstrate the robust soundness of these tests for large values of $e$, addressing a question raised by Dinur and Guruswami [Israel Journal of Mathematics, 209:611-649, 2015]. Previous soundness analyses of these tests were limited to scenarios where either $e=1$ or $k=1$. Even when $k=1$ and $e>1$, earlier analyses lacked robustness.

Furthermore, we explore a derandomized variant of the test, where the chosen polynomials $P_1, \dots, P_k$ can be a single, randomly selected polynomial $P$. This builds upon a result by Guruswami et al. [SIAM J. Comput., 46(1):132-159, 2017]. A crucial element in the proof of this robust soundness is an extension of the Schwartz-Zippel lemma to general finite fields $\mathbb{F}_q$, a result with potential independent significance.

When using heavy ions, such as carbon, in therapy, the beam breaks down into smaller fragments, generating secondary particles like protons and alpha particles.  Accurate estimations of the radiation dose delivered to tumors and surrounding healthy tissue require a precise understanding of the fluxes of these secondary fragments. Currently, available data on double differential carbon fragmentation cross sections—which are crucial for these estimations—is scarce, particularly within the energy range typically used in hadrontherapy (40 to 400 MeV/u). Consequently, new measurements have been conducted to determine the double differential cross section of carbon as it interacts with various thin targets. This paper details the results of an experiment conducted in May 2011 at GANIL. Specifically, we present measurements of the double differential cross sections and the angular distributions of the secondary fragments produced when 12C ions were fragmented at 95 MeV/u on thin targets made of carbon, CH2, aluminum, aluminum oxide, titanium, and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA). The experimental apparatus will be explained in detail, and a thorough assessment of systematic errors alongside all collected experimental data will be provided.
